_meta: Spec for the critical-minerals / battery-materials review package — the industry-review-packager skill's first born-spec package, built 2026-07-11 to serve ledger L024 (BATT Coalition, ultimate members undisclosed): map the field BATT files in so its co-lobby neighbors and the name-invisible players become visible. Facet CRITMIN, industry_lexicon.json v1.2. KPI values are human-verified copy — reconcile against the fresh CSVs after a corpus refresh.
title: Critical Minerals & Battery Materials — the Lobbying Field
subtitle: Who lobbies Washington on critical minerals, rare earths, and the battery-materials supply chain — the field where the BATT Coalition (a coalition whose ultimate members are not disclosed in its filings) sits, mapped from the filings' own free-text 2022–2026Q1.

press.regex: \b(critical minerals?|critical materials|rare earths?|battery materials?|battery supply chain|battery manufacturing|energy storage|lithium|graphite|foreign entity of concern)\b

overview: This package maps the federal lobbying field around critical minerals, rare earths, and the battery-materials supply chain — an industry that, like crypto, scatters across many disclosure issue codes (Natural Resources, Energy, Trade, Defense, Taxation, Manufacturing) and is therefore invisible to issue-code filtering alone. The map is built from what filers SAY they lobby on: 19 curated, collision-checked phrases (from 'critical minerals' and 'rare earths' to the statutory terms '45X', '30D', and 'foreign entity of concern') matched whole-word against the filings' own activity text.

One seed question this map serves: the Battery Advocacy for Technology Transformation (BATT) Coalition stood up ~$800K/yr of lobbying from a zero base in mid-2024 without disclosing its ultimate members. This package places BATT inside its field — the same-vocabulary co-filers (automakers, miners, chemical, defense and electronics companies, most with no battery/mineral term in their name) that a reporter would canvass to establish who funds and benefits from the coalition's asks. BATT files under two client-name spellings ('THE BATTERY ADVOCACY FOR TECHNOLOGY TRANSFORMATION (BATT) COALITION' via Strategic Marketing Innovations, and 'SMI ON BEHALF OF THE…' via Cannon|Pearce), which the entity resolver keeps as separate players — a documented name-resolution ceiling. A third similarly-named player, the 'BATTERY MATERIALS AND TECHNOLOGY COALITION' (via Venn Strategies, filing since 2022, $2.2M reported), appears to be a DISTINCT coalition in the same space — the field held an earlier battery-materials coalition before BATT emerged, and the two should not be conflated.
notes: This is unverified research output for team review. Headline numbers are candidates with record anchors, not locked findings; the candidate angles live in the investigation ledger (L024).

kpis.label: Client organizations on the map
kpis.note: 774 (91%) carry no battery/mineral term in their name — found only by what they say they lobby on

kpis.label: Tagged filings, 2025-Q4
kpis.note: vs 366 a year earlier (+28%); the field ran ~290/quarter through 2022

kpis.label: Canonical spend of tagged clients, 2025
kpis.note: all-issue spend of clients filing on the field that quarter — a size signal, not minerals dollars

kpis.label: BATT Coalition on the map
kpis.note: 18 tagged filings since 2024 via SMI + Cannon|Pearce · plus the distinct Battery Materials & Technology Coalition (Venn, since 2022) in the same field · BATT members undisclosed (ledger L024)

players.title: The player map — who lobbies on critical minerals and battery materials
players.sub: All {n} client-side players with CRITMIN-tagged senate filings 2022–2026Q1, sized by tagged filing count. Click a bubble to list its tagged filings.
players.foot: Recall-first map: most players carry NO battery/mineral term in their name — Ford, Rio Tinto, Edison Electric Institute, defense primes — and are found only by what their filings say. The BATT Coalition appears under two filed spellings, beside the distinct Battery Materials & Technology Coalition (see README). Incidental one-off mentions sit in the peripheral tier by design; a story names specific players from the CSVs, never 'the whole list.'

trend.title: A field that surged with the IRA credits
trend.sub: CRITMIN-tagged senate filings and distinct clients per quarter (amendment-deduped, registrations excluded). Click a quarter to list exactly the filings it counts.
trend.foot: A steady ~290 filings/quarter through 2022 climbs to 467 by 2025-Q4 (+28% year-over-year) with distinct clients up 246→401. The vocabulary is dominated by 'critical minerals', 'energy storage', the 45X/30D credits and 'rare earth' — statutory language of the 2022 IRA and the 2024–25 sourcing-restriction fights ('foreign entity of concern').

scatter.title: No single issue code holds this field
scatter.sub: Issue codes the registrants filed their CRITMIN-tagged free-text under (senate + house text blocks).
scatter.foot: The field scatters across Natural Resources, Energy, Trade, Defense, Taxation and Manufacturing — the same 'hidden under other codes' problem the crypto map demonstrated. This is why the BATT Coalition's field cannot be assembled from issue codes alone.

keywords.title: Which words carried the signal
keywords.sub: Distinct filings matched by each of the 19 curated lexicon phrases (whole-word, case-insensitive; industry_lexicon.json v1.2 facet CRITMIN).
keywords.foot: Collision-triaged: bare 'battery' (the wider EV/consumer-battery field), 'lithium-ion' (consumer fire-safety standards story), and 'cobalt' (Cobalt-60 medical sterilization) were checked and excluded — reasons recorded in the lexicon's display_only block.

registrants.title: The firms carrying the field's work
registrants.sub: Outside lobbying firms on CRITMIN-tagged filings (self-filers excluded), ranked by tagged filings.
registrants.foot: BATT's own firms — Strategic Marketing Innovations (SMI) and Cannon-Pearce — sit in this list; their other mineral-field clients are the natural first canvass for who else buys the same asks (ledger L024).

spendTop.title: The biggest lobbying budgets on the map
spendTop.sub: Mapped players ranked by TOTAL federal lobbying spend across all issues (canonical, double-count-corrected) — a size signal, not minerals dollars.
spendTop.foot: Filing-level disclosure cannot split dollars by issue; a diversified giant's budget is mostly not minerals work. Use with the tagged-filing counts on the map.

giving.title: Disclosed LD-203 giving of the field's top 150 players
giving.sub: The roster organizations' own disclosed LD-203 political giving (registrant-filed regime; amendment-deduped), with the per-member rollup (name variants + support committees merged; JFC/multi-honoree dollars stay unallocated).
giving.foot: Organization-level and NOT minerals-attributable: a diversified filer's giving reflects its whole portfolio. Super-PAC money is invisible in LD-203 by law and lives in FEC data — the two never sum.

press.title: The say side — members on critical minerals
press.sub: Share of ALL member press releases matching the critical-minerals vocabulary, by quarter. Click a quarter to list the releases.
press.foot: The share PEAKS in 2022-Q3 (1.49%) — the Inflation Reduction Act's passage quarter, when members touted the credits this field now lobbies on — then settles to a durable ~1% floor. Press counts are window-biased (the corpus starts 2022 and grows ~4× by 2025), so read the share line, not raw counts.

caveats: Recall-first map: any client whose filing free-text names one of the 19 curated phrases is included; incidental one-off mentions sit in the peripheral tier by design. A story names specific players from the CSVs, never 'the whole list.'
Spend figures are each player's TOTAL federal lobbying spend across all issues (canonical, double-count-corrected) — a size signal. Filing-level disclosure cannot split dollars by issue.

Senate filings are primary; House versions of the same filings are never added on top (they are copies). Filings are amendment-deduplicated on filing_period, latest by posted; registrations excluded from dollar work.

'Energy storage' includes non-battery storage (hydrogen, pumped hydro) — a documented recall/precision trade recorded in the lexicon; the mineral and battery-materials phrases are the high-precision core.

The BATT Coalition files under two client-name spellings which remain separate players on the map (the entity resolver's documented name-variant ceiling); the similarly-named 'Battery Materials and Technology Coalition' (Venn Strategies, filing since 2022) appears to be a DISTINCT organization in the same space — do not conflate the two.
LD-203 'disclosed giving' is the lobbyist-side regime only, organization-level, and never minerals-attributable. Super-PAC money legally lives in FEC data — the two never sum.
Everything is self-reported disclosure data. 'Disclosed' never means 'total': 501(c)(4) dark money and state lobbying are outside every number here.
